data.title: TriplePoint Venture: New Distribution Looks Unsustainable... Again
data.description:
It's déjà vu all over again.

Source: Yogi Berra

In March 2024, we opined that the dividend from TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (NYSE:TPVG) was unsustainable and the dividend cut was just two quarters away. The stock has certainly suffered since then and TPVG did indeed cut within that timeframe.

In August 2024, we once again opined that the new dividend was also unsustainable. Surprisingly that outlook ignored everything management said and relied on old fashioned math.

We left that article with the following words.

TPVG would still get an "Extreme" level of danger (50%-75%) of another distribution cut on our proprietary Kenny Loggins Scale.

We continue to rate this a "Sell"

Source: Author's Article August 8, 2024

As our followers know, the dividend cut risk rating is for one year and two days short of that, TPVG cut, again. Yea?

We look at the results and tell you how you should approach this.

Q2-2025

At the surface, things looked pretty good. The company delivered a total net investment income of 28 cents a share and total increase in assets (ignoring dividends paid) of 33 cents a share. Both did keep up the 30 cents of dividend, and that might have got the income investors happy for a brief moment. But, the company surprised the income chasing groupies with a cut.

The Board also made the decision to reduce our regular quarterly distribution of $0.23 per share. This was a difficult but we believe prudent decision that will enable us to better align our distribution levels as fundings under newer commitments gradually continue to materialize, and our historical prepayment activity remains present within the portfolio.

This decision also took into consideration the expectation of future rate cuts by the Fed as well as the higher interest rate environment we will face on refinancing our notes maturing in early 2026. We believe most importantly, this puts us in a more favorable position to over-earn our dividend regularly.

Source: TPVG Q2-2025 Conference Call Transcript

Just a note for the purists out there, we use dividends and distributions interchangeably and we will note that even TPVG did that in the paragraphs above. Ok, so they cut because they want to get ahead of the curve. That leaves us with a 13.3% yield and one that looks well covered as per the Q2-2025 statements at least. Well....No.

The key problem we have is that the TPVG BDC is still get whacked on actual cash flow. By that we mean that the dividend looks covered for Q2-2025, only on a non-cash basis. Payment-in-kind interest income was a whopping $5.25 million for Q2-2025. This compares to $3.82 million for Q2-2024 and about $3.757 million for Q1-2025. Investors tend to ignore these numbers or look at it as a percentage of investment income. Both are wrong ways to go about it. The correct way is to look at payment-in-kind in relation to net investment income of $11.275 million.

So real cash flow is $6.025 million a quarter. Applying that to roughly 40 million shares outstanding, gives us 15 cents a quarter. That is why the new 23 cents of dividend gets us about as excited as a decaffeinated latte.

Outlook & Verdict

TPVG missed the window to issue tons of stocks when it was trading well above NAV. Right now, it trades 20% below it, and stock issuance is confined to dividend reinvestment and that barely moves the needle.

Real cash flow is about 15 cents a quarter and possibly some of it can be converted into cash versus payment-in-kind. But we doubt it bridges the gap to 23 cents a share and eventually the BDC will have to deal with distressed market conditions and a recession. 16% of their debt investments currently rated as a "yellow" or "orange".

That 16% is fairly close to the investment income percentage that is currently paying via the payment-in-kind mechanism. So there are big problems ahead and we are at these levels even after full incentive fee waivers. If those come back at some point, you drop to 13 or 14 cents of sustainable distribution per share per quarter. All of this ignores any big losses in a recession.

Yes, the new 13% yield will likely draw in investors who have forgotten how TPVG went from a fully covered 40 cents per share to 15 cents over the last 3 years. The 20% discount to NAV may also draw some in.

We have to admit, that if we were in the early stages of an economic expansion, then some of these problems could be overlooked as a rising tide would fix even a leaky dinghy. But at this stage of the game? No way you should be chasing this. We would ignore everything south of the actual results including the extended waiver of incentive fees and the commitment of management to buy some stock. TPVG would still get an "Extreme" level of danger (50%-75%) of another distribution cut on our proprietary Kenny Loggins Scale.

We would continue to stay out or use any bounce to sell the stock.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it or sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Axos Financial: When A Premium Makes Sense
data.description:
Back in December 2023, I wrote an article about Axos Financial (NYSE:AX). At the time, the firm was making some interesting changes at the management level and was also making operational changes that included the acquisition of two loan portfolios from the FDIC at a meaningful discount to their actual value. I argued in that article that the historical growth of the business was impressive. And when you add on top of that the discount it was getting for the assets acquired, I believed that it justified a "Buy" rating.

Since then, the firm has continued to expand nicely. This has brought with it superior shareholder returns. Since my article on it, the stock is up 73.9%, handily outperforming the 37% increase that the S&P 500 has experienced. Looking at the picture today, I see that shares of the enterprise are no longer cheap. In fact, they are rather expensive. Admittedly, asset quality is really high as well. So I do think that deserves some wiggle room. Taking that into consideration, I believe that while the easy money has been made, Axos Financial still deserves a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

A fresh look at Axos Financial

In case you're not familiar with Axos Financial, the company is a rather decently sized financial institution with a market capitalization as of this writing of $4.83 billion. This is not just a traditional bank. As it has scaled, it has essentially become three separate businesses all rolled into one. The first of these would be its consumer banking division, which provides various financial products and services to everyday people. The second would be the commercial banking side that caters to businesses. And the third would be the securities business, which provides different investment and wealth management services to individual and institutional clients.

This diversification brings with its stability and, as you will see, high asset quality. But before we get to that, it would be important to cover the overall financial performance of the enterprise over the last few years now. Take deposits as an example. Although they're a liability, they're the literal fuel for growth for banks. From 2023 through 2025, deposits for the company expanded nicely from $17.12 billion to $20.83 billion. This is rather solid growth and came about despite that non-interest bearing deposits, which are the best kinds of deposits for a company like this, didn't really grow much.

As deposits increased, loans grew as well. They expanded from $16.46 billion in 2023 to $21.05 billion in 2025. Commercial real estate remains the largest exposure for the company, accounting for $6.94 billion, or approximately 32.2%, of its overall loan portfolio. But not terribly far behind would be the $6.80 billion dedicated to commercial and industrial loans. The firm does have a lot of exposure also to single family mortgage and warehouse loans, multifamily and commercial mortgage properties, and some to the automotive and consumer categories. I'm pleased with the fact that only about $380 million, or 1.8%, of its total loan portfolio involves office assets. That is amongst the lowest I have seen in the banking sector and brings me comfort at a time when office vacancy rates are high and rising.

Even though deposits and loans increased, other aspects of the balance sheet declined. As an example, securities have dropped from $233.1 million to $66.7 million over the last three years. Management has clearly made it a deliberate goal to focus more on loans and less on securities. At the same time, cash and cash equivalents also dropped, falling from $2.38 billion to $2.18 billion. The good news is that this decline was accompanied by a drop in the amount of debt that the firm has also. It ultimately fell from $611.6 million in 2023 to $512.1 million in 2025.

There are some other aspects of the company that deserve attention as well. In particular, the firm has a robust securities business that includes a massive amount of advisor services. At the end of the most recent year, the company had $28 billion in assets under custody. 57% of this involved its turnkey asset management platform where RIAs (Registered Investment Advisors) custody client assets with the firm and use its technology and trading capabilities to not only trade, but also assist with things like account openings, asset transfers, and more. 41% of the assets under custody that the firm has involves traditional RIAs that work with it. And the remaining 2% involves small RIAs.

This diverse and growing ecosystem has allowed overall revenue and profits for the institution to generally increase over the years. From 2023 to 2025, net interest income expanded from $758.9 million to $1.07 billion. On top of the increased balance sheet size, the changes that the company has made and the composition of its balance sheet has allowed net interest margin to grow from 4.35% to 4.90% over the last three years.

Non-interest income has been a bit more volatile. After rising from $120.5 million in 2023 to $222.7 million in 2024, it dropped quite a bit to $131.1 million in 2025. This can be explained, however, by the fact that, in 2024, the firm booked a $92.4 million gain on an acquisition. This involves the aforementioned loan portfolios that the company acquired from the FDIC that had an aggregate unpaid principal balance of $1.3 billion and a fair value of $901.5 million. It also included certain derivatives. If we strip this out from the picture, then non-interest income would have risen in each of the last three years.

Overall net profits have seen a similar path. After climbing from $307.2 million in 2023 to $450 million in 2024, there was a drop to $432.9 million in 2025. But if we strip out the after-tax acquisition gain that the firm booked for the 2024 fiscal year, net profits that year would have been only $384.6 million.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of Axos Financial are currently priced. On a price-to-earnings basis, the multiple of 11.2 is a bit higher than the 10 maximum that I like to see. However, even with that, the firm is cheaper than four of the five companies I compared it to in that chart. Unfortunately, this relative valuation advantage doesn't apply when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples as the chart below illustrates. In this case, the firm is trading well above both the book value and tangible book value that it has. It's also more expensive than four of the five competitors.

Typically, this would be a turn off as far as I'm concerned. But there's a big compensating factor here. That is the fact that asset quality for the company is top notch. What you see from more diverse and established financial institutions in the industry is that, as they become more diverse, asset quality improves. This is because traditional banking services are not that profitable. Generating additional revenue from non-interest activities can be a significant boon for shareholders.

In the chart above, you can see that the firm has a return on assets reading of 1.85%. That is massively above the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see. It also places the company above all five competitors that I stacked it up against in that chart. And in the chart below, you can see that, once again, Axos Financial comes out on top when it comes to return on equity. The 16.85% reading that the firm has is meaningfully above the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see. It's also comfortably above all of the five competitors.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I would argue that things are going quite well for Axos Financial. The company is growing nicely in most respects. Shares are decently priced relative to earnings, and asset quality is strong. Admittedly, the stock is more expensive than I would like it to be on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis. But when you consider all the other positives here, I believe the firm still deserves a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

data.title: Smith & Nephew: Doing Better And Still Offers Value
data.description:
Medical device manufacturer Smith & Nephew (NYSE:SNN) got a boost in London trading on Tuesday (5th August) after its interim results were well-received.

I last covered the share with my June 2024 “buy” note, Smith & Nephew: Work To Be Done But Still Long-Term Potential. Since then, it has moved up 34%.

My thesis a year ago was that the company was at its core well-positioned in an industry set to benefit over the long term from resilient demand and premium pricing potential, so as long as it could fix some medium-term performance issues seen in recent years, the share looked as if it offered value.

This week the company issued its interim results, making it an apposite moment to revisit the investment case.

2025 Started Solid, but Unspectacular

We already had some sense of how the business had been performing in 2025 thanks to April’s first quarter trading update.

That showed year-on-year revenue growth of 1.6%, with underlying growth of 3.1% reduced 1.5% by exchange rate movements. I see ongoing exchange rate fluctuations as a risk for the company given the current volatility of its reporting currency, the U.S. dollar.

All three operating divisions reported underlying revenue growth and the company maintained its full-year guidance. That includes underlying revenue growth around 5.0% (reported growth 5.4%), and trading profit margin expansion to between 19.0% and 20.0%. That is after expected net impact of $15 to $20 million from tariffs. Given the first quarter performance, the full-year target suggests management expects revenue growth to pick up in pace over the course of the year, although for now I do not see specific reasons to expect that to happen. One explanation may be that the company believes headwinds from China have now passed their peak, which could help comparative revenue numbers over the remainder of the year.

The interim results showed decent progress. Revenue was up 5% year-on-year, cash generated from operations 54% and free cash flow 528% (albeit from a low base).

Versus the first quarter discussed above, the second quarter saw (revenue) growth in all three business units and all regions ahead of the first quarter. I see that as positive in terms of what it signals about some of the company’s weaker-seeming prior spots, such as fixing the China business that had been seeing weaker demand (not a company-specific problem, but a problem nonetheless).

Dividend Growth Has Returned

Something to get excited about in the interim results was the announcement of a 4.2% increase in the interim dividend, to 15c per share.

The company also announced a $500m share buyback for the second half, which I see as a sign of management confidence in the company’s cash generation potential. That is positive, although personally I am not a fan of buybacks – I’d rather Smith and Nephew paid a higher dividend and/or invested for growth (or paying down its $2.7bn net debt, although I see that as perfectly manageable). Indeed in its capital allocation framework, surplus capital return to shareholders comes fourth, with “investing in the business to drive organic growth and meet our sustainability targets” and “invest(ing) in acquisitions, targeting new technologies in high growth segments with a strong strategic fit that meet our financial criteria” first and second. So the buyback announcement does not inspire me with confidence that management is sticking to its own capital allocation policy.

However, the dividend hike I see as significant because both interim and final dividends had been held flat since 2020 (a fuller dividend history is available here), prior to which the company had a broadly solid dividend growth track record that I discussed in my 2020 piece Smith & Nephew: A Long-Term Buy And Hold In Medical Devices.

In this week’s interim results, management said the company has a progressive dividend policy and “from 2025 onwards we expect a payout of around 35% to 40% of EPSA”.

Still, the current dividend yield of 2.1% is pretty unexciting in the current environment. It falls well short even of the U.K. blue-chip FTSE 100 index’s current average yield of 3.3%. If cash generation remains strong I would like to see management hike the payout quicker, making up for lost ground over the past five years and then some. For now, though, there is no indication that that is on the cards.

Recovery Story is Gathering Pace

I have long liked the investment case in principle though been unconvinced by the claimed expectations of the company’s 12-point plan. From its launch in 2021 I questioned why the company expected to meet demanding financial targets without evidence of real changes to drive that.

The first half of this year is not necessarily representative of the full year. Still, while the company bangs on in its report about how the 12-point plan is delivering I feel vindicated in my criticism.

When it outlined the plan in 2021, the firm was targeting consistent 4-6% organic revenue growth by 2024. The company did not break down organic revenue growth from its overall revenue growth in the interims. The second financial goal in the plan was “rebuilding trading margin, targeting at least 21% by 2024 with further improvements thereafter” – far ahead of the first half’s 17.7%.

I’m not surprised – I never thought the plan was realistic. I hope Smith and Nephew will learn from this. But even if I am underwhelmed on meeting the strategy’s targets, I do think the company has started to turn the ship around more generally and see the solid first half performance as further evidence of that.

It maintained its full-year outlook. It expects a net impact of $15-$20m from tariffs based on what is currently known. For the full year, the company’s expectation is underlying revenue growth of around 5.0% (reported growth 5.5%), and trading profit margin to expand to 19.0%-20.0%. That is a big jump in my view given that half the year is already over and the trading margin in it was 17.7%.

Stock Has Further to Go

I maintain my “buy” rating.

The London market greeted the interims very positively, with Smith & Nephew trading up around 13% higher than the prior day’s closing price.

That still only gives it a market cap of around £11.5bn, though. If we presume (and it is a presumption) that the first half is a baseline for the full year and beyond (the outlook suggests stronger performance, to note) then we are looking at full year cash flow from operations of around £855m. So the current enterprise value is around 16 times cash flow from operations.

I think that price is fair but not exciting. However, with the wind in its sails, it seems reasonable to expect earnings and free cash flow growth at the full-year level and certainly over the next several years, as business momentum builds. On that basis I continue to see the price as attractive given the underlying quality of the business in a sector with fairly resilient demand.

That might not happen, of course. Demand in China remains febrile, while tariffs are a big unknown. Exchange rate is another risk for the dollar-reporting, London-listed business.

Long term, though, Smith and Nephew still looks undervalued in my opinion.

data.title: Wolverine World Wide Q2: Growing Off Easy Comps And Saucony, But The Stock Is Pricey
data.description:
Wolverine World Wide, Inc. (NYSE:WWW) delivered an undeniably strong Q2, with revenues rising in the low-double digits and flagship brands performing exceptionally well.

However, results must be framed within context: the company is lapping a historically weak Q2 '24 and benefited from some timing shifts in wholesale due to tariff-related order pull-ins. Despite this, the improvement in brand health, gross margins, and operational execution is evident.

The market has responded with enthusiasm. The stock has nearly doubled since its April lows, and 75% since my last article in February (covering the Q4 '24 results). However, I believe the current valuation already reflects not just normalization, but a full return to peak revenues and margins.

Given Wolverine's historical cyclicality and uncertain consumer dynamics ahead, I maintain a Hold.

Q2 '25 results

A great quarter, off an easy base

Wolverine posted impressive headline figures, with revenues up 11%, gross margin up 410 bps, reaching 47.5%, adjusted operating margin up 300 bps, to 9.4%, and beating consensus.

However, this performance follows a dismal Q2 '24, where revenues declined 18.5% (adjusted closer to -21%), and following an even worse 1Q24 with the Active Group falling 25% YoY. So while this quarter marks a sharp rebound, it should be seen more as normalization than breakout growth.

There was also a $10 million shift in deliveries from Q3 '25 to Q2 '25 (not super important, but 20% of the growth), and probably retailer and distributor pull-forward of demand to cover from higher tariffs (although this last point was not commented on).

Gross margin improvements are part of a longer trend. After years of channel cleanup and excess discounting following the 2021 inventory glut, Wolverine has now stacked multiple quarters of margin recovery, even when sales were falling last year.

Still, even with this growth, the company remains below 2021 peaks in both revenue and profitability. Looking ahead, comps become tougher. Q3 '24 had much less dramatic declines (-7% YoY), and Q4 '24 already returned to growth (+3%). Management is guiding ~3% revenue growth for Q3 '25, and expectations moderate further into Q4 (no guidance).

Active brands breaking ground

The real story is in Active, especially Saucony, which grew a remarkable 41% YoY. After dropping 28% in the same period last year, this rebound puts the brand above 2023 and even 2021 levels and into record territory. Google Trends confirms that brand relevance is surging at very fast clips (U.S. and Global). Saucony is now almost the largest brand of the company, and 25/30% of revenues.

Merrell also posted a solid 11% growth after being down 20% last year, and therefore, is still below peak levels (in 2022 it did more than $200 million in revenues in Q2, versus $158 million in 2025).

International momentum is strong for both brands, with EMEA and APAC growing above the U.S. Merrell, for example, grows more in search abroad than in the U.S.

If this trend continues, Merrell could emulate breakout success stories like Hoka from Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK).

These brands are outperforming expectations, and management is investing heavily behind them, both in upper-funnel marketing and in physical door expansion. The company believes they’re in the "early innings" of their growth story (call).

On the other hand, Sweaty Betty continues to underperform, with sales down YoY. The Work Group (CAT license and Wolverine brand) was up in the low-single digits, better than feared, but still modest. These brands appear to be stabilizing, but they are unlikely to drive meaningful growth or margin expansion.

Not that much operating leverage

One of the most attractive aspects of growth in a consumer brand is operating leverage, when sales and gross profits improve faster than SG&A. But Wolverine's SG&A rose 20% YoY in Q2, faster than revenues. Operating margin still improved because gross margins grew a lot, but the driver was not sales growth.

Management explained this by pointing to strategic reinvestments: talent upgrades, digital infrastructure, integrated planning tools, and brand marketing. While these are likely important long-term investments, they limit earnings upside.

Deleveraging the balance sheet

Wolverine has made good progress on deleveraging. Net debt is down, and the company expects to generate $150M in operating cash flow this year. Capex remains modest, so there is room to continue reducing debt or building a cash cushion ahead of a potentially more challenging macro backdrop.

Valuation is already at peak expectations

Wolverine has become an optimistic (therefore expensive) stock, given where it sits relative to historical performance.

Let’s take some scenarios, based on the company's historical performance.

First, a moderate recovery to $2.1 billion in revenue, 9% operating margins, leading to $190 million in EBIT. Minus $35 million in interest and 25% tax, this leads to about $120 million in net income.

A bull case could be a return to the 2021 or 2015 peak, at $2.7 billion in revenues, and an 11% margin (the company believes it can reach mid-teens, but it has not been able to in the past). This implies about $300 million in operating income and $200 million in net income.

With the stock at ~$27 and market cap around $2.2 billion, that implies: 18x earnings on the base case, and ~11x earnings on the most optimistic scenario, which assumes both peak revenue and peak margin return.

For a consumer discretionary business still below its previous peak, in a volatile macro environment and facing tariff headwinds, this valuation feels stretched.

Especially considering that Wolverine hasn’t sustainably surpassed its 2015-2021 growth ceiling, and that operating leverage may be limited by ongoing brand investments.

Wolverine is recovering, especially in its high-potential Active brands. Saucony and Merrell which are delivering real momentum and may deliver growth above the previous peaks.

But at the current price, the market appears to be pricing in a full recovery for Wolverine World Wide, Inc., or even more. The stock is already discounting a return to peak revenue and margin levels, and then some. In a sector that remains cyclical and sensitive to tariffs and consumer confidence, that’s a big ask. I prefer to wait for a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Linde Q2 2025 Earnings: Resilient Growth In A Challenging Environment
data.description:
Executive Summary

Linde (NASDAQ:LIN) continues to demonstrate resilience in this challenging environment. The company reported revenue of $8.5 billion for Q2 2025, up 3% YoY. Adjusted EPS came in at $4.09, up 6% YoY. The company saw a healthy operating cash flow of $2.2 billion, up 15% YoY.

The guidance came in strong for the third quarter, with the company expecting adjusted EPS to fall within the range of $4.10 to $4.20, representing a 4% to 7% increase YoY. The full-year guidance for adjusted EPS was provided to fall in the range of $16.30 to $16.50, representing a 5% to 6% increase YoY.

It is important to note that management raised the low-end of the full-year guidance from $16.20 to $16.20. Although the top-end range remains at $16.50, management clarified that the top-end assumes economic contraction. Thus, I believe the management to be conservative here, given my belief that the economy would strengthen as the year progresses.

The company has a $7.1 billion gas sale backlog. The company has very stringent criteria for classifying backlogs. To be included as a backlog, the project must exhibit incremental growth and must be secured by contractual fixed fees with high-quality customers. Also, contract renewals and plants without customer commitments are not included in the backlog. Thus, one can be assured by this $7.1 billion backlog to assess future growth.

In my opinion, the revised guidance demonstrates management's confidence going into the second half of this year. This is better than the last time I covered Linde. In my previous article, I talked about Linde's strong pricing power, which continues to prevail in this challenging economy. There appears to be some evidence that Europe can recover in the coming years. I provide my thoughts on this in the section from EMEA below.

Thus, I believe the road ahead appears to be less rocky than what it was at the time of my prior publication. However, I want to see meaningful positive developments, especially with the Russia-Ukraine conflict. Thus, I am maintaining my price target of $500 and reiterating my Buy rating. I will explain my thesis in the following sections.

Investment Thesis: Look into the segments

Americas

This is the most resilient segment of all segments. The company expects volumes to be flat in the near term. However, there are tailwinds that can eventually increase volumes. The company caters to various end-markets, some of which are defensive and some are cyclicals. The Q2 2025 sales by end-market are provided below:

A big portion of the end market is the defensive sector (healthcare, food & beverages). Most of these sectors should see positive growth in the near to long term. I would like to focus on the manufacturing sector here. This sector has been a drag to the volume growth, as we all know how bad this sector is doing today.

However, CEO Sanjiv Lamda pointed out that the commercial space market sits in this sector. There is a need for ultra-pure fuels and cooling liquids like liquid nitrogen. In fact, the company announced major U.S. investments to support this sector. This facility should be up by the first quarter of 2027. Additionally, the company is also expected to start a new air separation unit in Texas by the first quarter of 2026.

With space missions ramping up in the U.S., I expect this segment to do exceptionally well by the end of 2028-2029. The company is also targeting a 30-50bp margin expansion. Thus, with all these considerations, I have projected the revenue growth for this sector as follows:

APAC

The APAC (Asia Pacific) segment looks promising. The two important economies, China and India, fall under this segment. The sales by end-market for this region are shown below:

According to Invest India, electronics and semiconductors is the second fastest-growing sector in the country. The semiconductor market is expected to grow by 13% to reach $103 billion in 2030. Electric Vehicles (EV) and the food processing industries also make the top 7 cut, with EV ranking at #5 and food processing at #7. These should drive strong double-digit volume growth for Linde. According to IBISWorld, the fastest-growing sectors in China are cafes, restaurants, and EVs. There is also strong government stimulus in China to revamp its sluggish economy. Thus, I expect decent growth for this segment in the near to midterm. With all these considerations, I have projected the revenue growth for this sector as follows:

EMEA

The EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) segment is concerning due to the slowdown in economic activity and geopolitical tensions. The growth from all the defensive sectors will unfortunately be offset by the declining industrial sectors in the near to mid-term. The sales by end market for this region are shown below:

If you see this, a big portion of sales come from cyclical sectors, and that is precisely the region why volume growth for this quarter was -4%. The silver lining here is that the drop in volume is being offset by Linde's pricing power and the FX fluctuations (weak dollar). This segment too has potential tailwinds brewing that could take effect in the next 4-5 years. For instance, Politico reports that:

The German parliament recently passed a constitutional reform that allows far greater rearmament spending. Incoming chancellor Friedrich Merz's governing coalition, set to start work next month, agreed to a €1 trillion package of military and infrastructure investment.

This massive stimulus will take shape in the coming years, and Linde should benefit from this. Likewise, Linde has been operating in Ukraine for the past 3 years. Any resolution to the ongoing conflict with Russia should be beneficial for Linde as Ukraine rebuilds itself. Thus, with all these considerations, I have projected the revenue growth for this sector as follows:

Engineering

This business segment focuses on either supplying industrial plant components to end customers or to other industrial gas businesses of Linde. In general, this segment focuses on market segments like air separation, hydrogen, natural gas plants, etc. Linde's proprietary technology helps customers capture carbon dioxide emissions, helping in their sustainability efforts. I don't expect the company to maintain the 7.5% growth rate from 2024 since some of this was attributed to project timings as per their 2024 10K. I am forecasting a modest 2% growth primarily driven by cost savings and process improvements at Linde. I have projected the revenue growth for this sector as follows:

Global Others

The final segment consists of smaller businesses and corporate costs that are lumped together here. The company categorizes helium wholesale under this segment. There has been excess helium supply in the market, causing global helium prices to fall. However, the management at Linde has reiterated that the helium business is very small and this surplus supply should not have material impact. I have projected the revenue growth for this sector as follows:

Total Revenue Forecast

The consolidated revenue forecast for Linde is shown below:

The above forecast is very conservative with the projections at or below Wall Street estimates. My projections are much lower than the consensus estimates on Seeking Alpha. I feel good about this projection, as this helps me to be conservative.

Fundamental Analysis

If you are into numbers like me, feel free to read through my DCF analysis. If you are short on time, feel free to skip to the end of the section, where I provide the sensitivity analysis.

Income Statement Forecast

Using the above revenue growth rates, I constructed the three-statement model as depicted in this section. The company is expected to grow operating margins despite a challenging environment. This speaks volumes about the quality of management at Linde. In fact, Linde scores an A+ in Profitability, as per Seeking Alpha Quant ratings. It does fall behind in valuation.

Discounted Cash Flow Valuation

I have summarized my DCF calculations in the graphic below. Please comment if you have any questions about this.

WACC

I always highlight in all my articles that the WACC is the most controversial section, and small changes in the WACC can have a huge impact on valuations. Thus, I also provide a sensitivity analysis on various WACCs on the equity value.

Cost of debt: 4.0%

Risk-free rate: 4.2%

Tax rate: 23.36% (I am not accounting for any additional tax breaks. Also, I am not accounting for accelerated depreciation benefits from the passing of the Big Beautiful Bill)

Cost of debt (after tax) = 4.0% * (1-23.36%) = 3.07%

Beta: 0.93

Market risk premium: 4.33%

Cost of equity = Rfr + Beta x MRP = 8.40%

Equity: $220,852.3

Debt: $22,592.0

WACC: 7.86%

The valuation comes out to be $372.18 for the perpetuity method and $399.52 using the Exit EBITDA method, where I used the industry median EBITDA multiple of 17.0x.

Now, this is far below the current share price. However, my revenue projections are at or below the lower end of analyst estimates. As part of my sensitivity analysis, projections closer to analyst estimates bring the equity price to around $450.

Valuation: Conservative Approach

I want readers to understand that valuation is very sensitive to growth rates. Now, just to see how conservative I was, I constructed a "Bull Case" thesis that matches consensus estimates. I will provide just a snapshot of key information:

Now using consensus revenue, this puts this well over where the stock price is trading today. That is why I said earlier that valuation is not as bad as it seems. This shows how important sensitivity analysis is.

Sensitivity Analysis

The following table shows a sensitivity analysis for the "Bull Case," with growth rates ranging from 4.0% to 5.0% and a WACC from 7.36% to 8.36%. As this is my Bull Case, it is prudent for me to use the lower-end values, thus putting my price target at $500.

Technical Analysis

I generally provide a short analysis using Hurst Cycle analysis. But there have been no major trend changes since the publication of my previous article. I would like to just add that the ascending wedge pattern that I had highlighted still seems to be intact (although there was a recent breakdown, but the stock recovered quickly).

Bear Case

Recovery in the EMEA segment is crucial for revenue growth. Lack of economic recovery in Europe (especially in Germany) can put a sustained downward pressure on volumes. The Russia-Ukraine war needs to end soon. Lack of resolution can stall Linde's growth in this region.

The recovery of the Manufacturing segment in the US is important. As noted earlier, commercial space exploration falls under this segment. Any slowdowns in this section due to political issues in the U.S. can slow down the recovery in the Americas segment.

I also want to highlight that I am purposefully not including tariff escalations in my bear case. This is because management has time and again proved that they have exceptional pricing capabilities. Management explained in the Q2 2025 earnings call that products from Linde are a very small portion of the cost for end-customers. Therefore, they do not push back on price hikes by Linde since the end-customer's priority is uninterrupted supply of industrial gases for its processes.

Conclusion

The sale of the gas backlog of $7.1 billion instills confidence in me. As noted before, the management has very stringent criteria for categorizing backlogs. Of this $7.1 billion backlog, about $5.3 billion is in the Americas. This segment has shown resilience, and thus the risk is minimal.

With global economic tailwinds (such as the German and Chinese government stimulus, a strong Indian economy, etc.), the company is well positioned to capture growth. Thus, I am reiterating my Buy rating and my price target of $500.

data.title: Argan: A Compelling Buy With High-Risk, High-Reward Potential
data.description:
The demand for power in the US is growing rapidly for the first time in decades, thanks to the massive growth in data centers and Electric Vehicle [EV] charging. In 2024, electricity consumption increased by 128 TWh (vs. 2023), that's more than 2X New York City's annual consumption. This surge in power demand is a huge opportunity for Argan, Inc. (AGX), which specializes in the construction of new power plants. However, the company's success hinges entirely on its ability to secure new contracts in a competitive environment. In this article, I explain why I think AGX has a high-risk, high-reward profile but could be a worthy bet for investors with a high-risk tolerance. Large gains are possible if it capitalizes on the growing energy demand, but significant losses could occur if the company fails to win new business.

Argan, Inc. is one of the leading engineering, procurement, and construction [EPC] firms specializing in natural gas and renewable energy power plants, including solar and wind. The company operates three segments: power industry services, industrial construction services, and telecommunications infrastructure services. However, the power industry services segment is the largest revenue driver, contributing more than 80% of Q1 2026 revenues, and is expected to grow at a faster pace than the other segments.

New Era of Growth in U.S. Power Demand

AGX's growth prospects are bolstered by surging U.S. power demand, largely driven by the rapid expansion of AI data centers. AI data centers are power-hungry beasts, some of the largest facilities now require more than 1 gigawatt, enough to power a city of 2 million people. Moreover, several AI giants, including Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, and Meta, are planning to build multi-gigawatt data centers in the near term. As such, power demand from data center growth is forecast to more than double over the next five years. These companies are also investing in large-scale power generation for their exclusive use, creating additional opportunities for EPC contractors. Beyond data centers, the current federal policies have spurred a new wave of local manufacturing and re-industrialization, while the EV charging infrastructure continues to expand. Together, these trends represent a significant tailwind for AGX’s core business.

Opportunities In Natural Gas Plant Construction

Another important trend for AGX is the ongoing transition toward cleaner energy sources. In 2007, coal accounted for about 50% of U.S. electricity generation, but this allocation was down to 15% by 2024. Over the same period, natural gas has grown from 21% to 43%, as aging coal plants are replaced with cleaner natural gas facilities. Natural gas is nearly 50% cleaner than coal and much more reliable than wind or solar. Solar and wind are cleaner alternatives, but their output depends on weather, thus often failing to meet peak demand surges. Natural gas is therefore preferred to meet the demand surges beyond the limits of renewable sources. This trend presents a big opportunity for AGX, as building natural gas plants is more complex, less competitive, and generates significantly more revenue than solar or wind projects.

Competitive Position

Although AGX has no clear competitive advantage over other EPC companies, it has consistently secured large-scale EPC contracts for advanced natural gas power plants such as the 1,875 MW Guernsey Power Station, the 950 MW Trumbull Energy Center, and the 1.2 GW Sandow Lakes Power Station. It is building technical expertise and credibility, strengthening its position to compete for even larger contracts.

Prominent competitors like PWR and MTZ have significantly larger revenue bases, but they operate more broadly across both energy generation and transmission. In contrast, AGX maintains a more focused strategy, concentrating on power generation. Despite its smaller size, AGX achieves higher margins and returns on investment than these competitors. Its smaller revenue base also allows for more rapid top-line growth, if it secures new large contracts. Additionally, AGX has no long-term debt on its balance sheet, giving it the financial flexibility to pursue large-scale projects.

Valuation

AGX is currently trading at a forward P/E ratio of 34.4, which is pretty close to the industry average of 32.7. Analyst projections indicate that EPS will grow from $6.15 in FY2025 to $9.95 by FY2028. While there is significant uncertainty around these projections, they provide a useful basis for valuation. In my model, I multiplied the FY2028 EPS estimate by the industry average P/E to calculate an implied future share price of $325.37. I then discounted this figure back to present value using a discount rate [WACC] of 11.3%, which was obtained from the required return on equity calculated using the capital asset pricing model. This calculation used a Beta of 1.1 for AGX, an S&P 500 average annual return of 10.6% based on historical annual returns in the last 50 years, and a risk-free rate of 4.2%. The model also assumes that AGX is 100% equity-financed, as its total debt of $2.68M is significantly lower than its market capitalization of $3.18 billion (see table below).

The model yielded an implied current share price of $236, which is roughly in line with the current stock price. I am very cautious about this valuation, given the risks I will highlight below, but it shows that the company is fairly valued based on current expectations.

Backlog And Risks

The major indicator of AGX’s prospects is the project backlog, which represents the value of contracts for projects yet to be completed. As an EPC company, AGX bids on power plant construction contracts, and once awarded, the expected revenue is added to the backlog. These projects often span several years, with revenue recognized gradually and subtracted from the backlog over time as different phases are completed. The backlog thus provides visibility into future revenues.

The biggest risk to AGX is that its backlog and revenues are heavily concentrated in a few large projects. For instance, it was recently awarded a contract to build the 1.2 GW Sandow Lakes Power Station, which added roughly $0.5 billion to the project backlog last quarter, bringing the total backlog to $1.86 billion. This is more than 25% exposure to one project.

Typically, the company adds projects to the backlog only after receiving a notice to proceed from the project owner, so there’s a high level of certainty that revenues will materialize. However, projects can still be cancelled, causing a significant overnight drop in the backlog and a corresponding decline in stock price. Notably, several large projects in the backlog are expected to be completed in 2025–2026 (highlighted in the figure below). Once those wrap up, the backlog will shrink unless they manage to land new contracts. A large decline in backlog, whether due to project completion or cancellation, could trigger a sharp selloff of the stock.

The second major risk is that the growing power demand doesn’t necessarily translate to new power plant construction. Most of the 128 TWh demand increase from last year was satisfied by increased utilization of existing plants, not new construction. Most of AGX’s revenue comes from the construction of new power plants, not from their operation or maintenance. Thus, an increase in utilization doesn’t help AGX directly. Over time, as existing plants become more efficient or better utilized, demand for new plant construction may slow down and limit AGX's growth. It's difficult to predict exactly when this might happen, but it's a legitimate concern.

Conclusion: High Risk, High Reward

AGX’s core business and stock performance will continue to depend on its ability to secure new EPC contracts. One large-scale project can add $0.5 billion or more to the backlog, just like the Sandow Lakes Power Station. If they secure two to three additional large-scale projects within the next 1-2 years, the backlog may grow to over $3 billion, driving the stock price up by 2–3 times the current value. On the flip side, if the current projects conclude without the company securing new contracts, both the project backlog and stock price could fall by more than 50%. However, I believe that the odds favor AGX securing new contracts and expanding its project backlog. Industry analysts estimate that about 120 new gas power plants with a total capacity of 80 GW will be built by 2030. Even though AGX lacks a distinct competitive edge, this surge in planned capacity is a rising tide that could lift all players in the sector, including AGX.

The decision to invest in AGX ultimately comes down to risk appetite. It’s a high-risk, high-reward opportunity that could go either way. However, given the favorable industry outlook and the company’s track record in securing large contracts, it’s more likely that the company will continue to grow its backlog, driving the stock price higher.
